title: Post-call: Credentials strategy + SDK handoff for Finly POC
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
To: Morgan Blake <morgan@finly.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 10 Jun 2027 15:20:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call: Credentials strategy + SDK handoff for Finly POC

Hi Morgan — thanks again for the time today. Quick recap + proposed next steps so we can get your engineers started this week:

Summary of call
- Goal: POC Redwood inference for customer support reranking and a tool-calling agent in front of the knowledge base.
- Preferred routing: US region, goal is sub-150ms median token latency for short queries.
- Security ask: short-lived credentials, automated rotation, and evidence for key revocation.

Open questions we promised to answer
1) Recommended auth pattern for your infra (API key vs service account / OAuth).
2) How key rotation and audit logs fit into your compliance requirements.
3) SDK onboarding steps for your devs + a small sample integration.

What we recommend (quick version)
- For the POC: service-account-based flow with short-lived JWT exchange (OIDC/JWT or STS style) that grants temporary Redwood tokens. This meets your rotation/audit goals and avoids long-lived plaintext API keys in customer code.
- We'll provide an SDK quickstart (attached) with a sample service-account exchange and a tiny routing example that supports a fallback to our lighter LLM when high throughput is required.

Next actions
- I attached the SDK Quickstart and a draft mutual action plan (MAP). Can you confirm the security contact we should include for legal/SOC evidence? You mentioned Priya might own this — copying Priya here.
- If you're good with the service account approach, Ben (cc) will send a short config snippet and a suggested rotation cadence.
- Proposed follow-up: 30-min technical handoff tomorrow 10:00am PT to run through the quickstart. Does that work?

Thanks — Ava

--
Ava Chen
Sales Engineer | Redwood Inference
ava_chen@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0123

Attachments:
- redwood-sdk-quickstart.pdf (SDK quickstart with example token exchange and sample app)
- mutual-action-plan-draft.docx (POC milestones, success metrics, timeline)

From: Morgan Blake <morgan@finly.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Shah <pshah@ledgercorp.io>
Date: Thu, 10 Jun 2027 16:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: Credentials strategy + SDK handoff for Finly POC

Thanks Ava — that recap is helpful. Priya here (security at LedgerCorp / Finly partner), happy to join. A few clarifications from our side:

- Preference: we strongly prefer short-lived credentials tied to our identity provider (we use Okta). Ideally we want an OIDC-based flow where our CI/service system exchanges a signed assertion for a Redwood token.
- Rotation: our policy is 24-72 hour short-lived tokens for service flows, and strict rotation for any long-lived keys (rotate quarterly).
- Audit: we will need token issuance logs and key rotation events for the SOC review. Can your platform produce a simple CSV of token events for a given service account?

Tomorrow at 10:00am PT works for a quick demo. Please include a brief architecture slide showing how the JWT exchange, temporary token lifetime, and audit stream look.

-Morgan

On Thu, Jun 10, 2027 at 3:20 PM Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Morgan — thanks again for the time today. Quick recap + proposed next steps so we can get your engineers started this week:
> ...


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Morgan Blake <morgan@finly.com>, Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Priya Shah <pshah@ledgercorp.io>
Date: Fri, 11 Jun 2027 08:14:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: Credentials strategy + SDK handoff for Finly POC (recommended pattern)

Morgan / Priya — quick technical notes to align on the recommended pattern and what we'll include in the quickstart:

Recommended credentials flow (POC -> production-ready)- 1) Create a Redwood service account mapped to an Okta OIDC client (your identity team can mint signed assertions).
- 2) Use a short-lived JWT / OIDC assertion to call Redwood's token-exchange endpoint: POST /v1/auth/exchange -> returns a time-limited Bearer token (recommended TTL: 1–12 hours depending on workload).
- 3) SDK uses the Bearer token for inference calls; Redis-backed prefix/KV caching on our side still works the same.

Key rotation + audit- Our platform supports rotating the service-account private keys and will emit rotation events to audit logs. We can export token issuance and key rotation events as CSV/JSON (we'll attach a sample export in the next email). For SOC evidence, we can also provide a signed timestamped report for the POC window.

Routing / structured output notes- For your reranker + tool-calling agent, we recommend: route short, latency-sensitive queries to the dedicated pool (sub-150ms target) and batch lower-priority reranking jobs to a cost-optimized pool.
- We support structured output with schema validation and tool-calling hooks in the SDK. The service-account token scopes can be limited per-route (read-only embeddings vs generate with tools).

Example exchange snippet (pseudo-config)
- service_account: finly-poc-sa
- oidc_provider: https://okta.company.com
- token_ttl_seconds: 3600
- allowed_routes: ["/v1/generate","/v1/embeddings"]

I'll post a small sample token issuance log and a one-pager on rotation after the call. Ben

--
Ben Carter
Solutions Architect | Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com

Attachments referenced: service-account-policy-template.pdf (policy template for service accounts)

From: Ava Chen <ava_chen@redwood.com>
To: Morgan Blake <morgan@finly.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Priya Shah <pshah@ledgercorp.io>
Date: Fri, 11 Jun 2027 09:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: Credentials strategy + SDK handoff for Finly POC

Agenda (30m)
- 5m: Introductions & POC goals
- 10m: Walkthrough of SDK quickstart and the OIDC exchange flow (Ben)
- 10m: Audit/rotation evidence and next steps for SOC (Ava)
- 5m: Action items and MAP sign-off

Pre-call checklist for Finly
- Confirm an Okta OIDC client ID we can use for the POC (send to ava_chen@redwood.com).
- Confirm preferred token TTL (we suggested 1 hour for POC).
- Share any existing ingress IP allowlist if you want us to restrict the token-exchange endpoint for the POC window.

I'll bring the MAP draft and the service-account policy template to the call (attachments included). If Priya wants to pre-review the SOC evidence format, Ben can post the sample export before the call.

title: Deadline breach: cordon & recovery plan for ACME evaluation
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Priya Singh <priya.singh@acme.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 7 Jun 2027 09:15:00 -0700
Subject: Deadline breach: cordon & recovery plan for ACME evaluation

Hi Priya —

Quick update from last night's acceptance run: the final eval set hit a regression in multi-turn context handling that causes the acceptance criteria to fail intermittently. We ran a focused repro overnight and confirmed the root cause is related to a tokenizer edge-case when system prompts contain the customer metadata header.

Given that this directly impacts the close timeline, we recommend an immediate cordon (freeze) on any non-critical changes into the eval path and the following recovery steps:

- Cordon window: effective now until we confirm a stable acceptance run (expected 48–72 hours)
- Triage & owner: Jonas to drive triage with the SRE/infra patch; Grace to coordinate test permutations and acceptance harness updates
- Daily sync: 10:00 PT standup (15m) with customer leads and our engineers until green
- MAP update: propose moving Acceptance Complete milestone +10 business days, provisional — we need your buy-in to lock the calendar
- Escalation: if we don't reach stable pass within 72 hours, we will escalate to your procurement lead for a formal exception request and to our sales leader for credit/SLAs

I attached the acceptance-fail-log.csv and a short recovery-plan-v1.pdf with the proposed triage timeline. Can you confirm availability for the daily 10:00 PT sync and whether Tom should be looped in on procurement-side contingency?

Apologies for the late notice — want to own this and get us back to the target close without surprise.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Principal Customer Success Manager
Redwood Inference

Attachments: acceptance-fail-log.csv (text/csv), recovery-plan-v1.pdf (application/pdf)

From: Priya Singh <priya.singh@acme.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@acme.com>
Date: Mon, 7 Jun 2027 11:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Deadline breach: cordon & recovery plan for ACME evaluation

Ava —

Thanks for the quick heads up and for owning the recovery. This is concerning given our internal go-live commitments. I can join the 10:00 PT standups. I would like a short write-up on the exact acceptance test that failed (input + expected/actual) so our QA can review in parallel.

Tom: looping you in to confirm whether the +10 business day provisional milestone is acceptable from ACME procurement's perspective, or if we should start formalizing a timeline exception.

Priya
Director, Product Operations
ACME Corporation

On Mon, Jun 7, 2027 at 09:15 Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com> wrote:
> [quoted message omitted]


From: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
To: Priya Singh <priya.singh@acme.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 8 Jun 2027 08:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Deadline breach: cordon & recovery plan for ACME evaluation

Priya / Ava —

Short technical update and the concrete asks from engineering:

1) Repro & fix ETA
- We reproduced the tokenizer edge-case locally; root cause is a rare token-split on injected metadata (happens ~0.6% of runs with current eval set)
- Fix: small tokenizer normalization + a hotfix to the eval harness to strip/escape the problematic header — we expect a build and can run a staged acceptance by tomorrow EOD (Wed 2027-06-09) if we get approval to proceed

2) Required access / approvals
- Need permission to run a limited canary in your staging environment (read-only logs only).
- If acceptable, please confirm staging hostname and the service account we should use; Grace will coordinate test harness changes

3) MAP and timeline recommendation
- Proposed MAP change: Acceptance Complete -> 2027-06-22 (previously 2027-06-10) — this is conservative to give buffer for intermittent flakiness and a full regression pass
- We will publish an updated MAP spreadsheet after the first green run; Marcus will attach the updated calendar

4) Communication cadence
- Daily 10:00 PT sync (15m) — quick blocker list + owner/ETA
- Status note to be sent to Tom and your execs at 18:00 PT daily until fixed

Ava — please confirm the canary in their staging and coordinate the 10:00 standups. Priya / Tom — please confirm if the +12 business day rebaseline (to 2027-06-22) is acceptable; if procurement needs a formal exception doc, we can push that by EOD today.

Thanks,
Jonas Weber
Director, Reliability Engineering
Redwood Inference

> On Mon, Jun 7, 2027 at 11:30 Priya Singh <priya.singh@acme.com> wrote:
> > [quoted message omitted]


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Priya Singh <priya.singh@acme.com>, Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@acme.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 10 Jun 2027 16:40:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Deadline breach: cordon & recovery plan for ACME evaluation

Priya / Tom —

Following the successful staged acceptance this morning (we saw the pass rate climb from 99.4% to 99.98% on the canonical eval), we've attached an updated MAP calendar (updated-map-20270610.xlsx) and a slightly tightened recovery runbook. Key points:

- New acceptance milestone: 2027-06-21 (we trimmed one day after the green run, still includes buffer)
- Root cause fix: deployed to staging and validated; fix will be in the next PR to prod gate (scheduled Saturday maintenance)
- Credits/SLAs: we will hold off on formal credit conversations unless a further regression occurs; we documented the contingency for procurement in the attachment

Next steps for ACME:
- Please confirm acceptance of the 2027-06-21 milestone by EOD tomorrow so we can update internal calendars and Ops-on-call rota
- If confirmation is not received by EOD, per our escalation policy we will escalate to my manager and your procurement lead for an exception/credit decision

I appreciate the patience — team did a focused job to limit the slip and keep the MAP changes minimal. Drive-by notes: Grace will publish the test permutations and the acceptance harness diffs to the Drive link in the related_links field.

Marcus Lin
Senior Program Manager, Customer Success
Redwood Inference

Attachments: updated-map-20270610.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet)

- Functional: model responses deterministic within acceptable fuzz, no sensitive config leakage in logs

- Security: SOC2 evidence checklist items provided, network flow logs verified, KMS test pass

title: Deployment pathway scenarios & sizing proforma
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Elena Park <elena@novapay.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-10T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Post-call recap + proforma request (hosted vs dedicated vs private)

Hi Elena — thanks again for the call this morning. Quick recap and next steps so we're aligned on sizing and deployment pathway options: 

1) What we heard from NovaPay:
- Primary use: tokenization + conversational support inside your merchant dashboard for fraud triage and dispute summaries.
- Peak TPS target: ~250 short-completion calls per second during batch settlement windows (peaks last ~10–15 min). Off-peak ~30 TPS.
- Token mix: average request ~220 input tokens, 140 output tokens; 95thpct requests up to 1,200 tokens (long receipts + context).
- Latency target: p95 < 350ms for short completions, p95 < 1.2s for long-context ops.
- Data residency: US-only; SOC2 evidence required.

2) Deployment pathways we discussed:
- Hosted API: fastest to production but shared pool; recommend only for non-sensitive early rollout.
- Dedicated capacity: reserved GPU pools (best predictability for your TPS bursts), supports KV/prefix caching to lower cost on repeated contexts.
- Private VPC/on-prem: for eventual regulated workflows; longer lead time and integration work.

3) Sizing asks we need from you to produce a proforma (we added a basic worksheet attached):
- Clarify percent of calls that are short (<400 tokens) vs long (>400 tokens) during peak.
- Any expected concurrency from your frontend (concurrent open sessions interacting with chat/generate API)?
- Confirmation if 99% of requests will be within US region.

Next steps: if you can confirm the short/long mix and typical concurrency by Friday, we'll return a dedicated-capacity proforma (GPU mix options + cost/latency tradeoffs) and a hosted fall-back plan.

Attachments: I attached the blank proforma and our SOC2 summary for procurement.

Cheers,
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

--
Ava Chen
Redwood Inference
ava@redwood.com
(415) 555-0192


From: Elena Park <elena@novapay.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-10T10:07:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + proforma request (hosted vs dedicated vs private)

Hi Ava — thanks for the tidy recap. Confirming the numbers from our end: 

- Peak TPS window: 250 TPS (as discussed). We expect this to be concentrated in three 12–15 minute windows per day.
- Token mix estimate (rough): ~65% short (<=400 tokens), ~30% medium (401–800), ~5% long (>800).
- Average concurrency: frontend keeps ~1.8k concurrent open sessions during peak; estimate ~15% of those actively sending requests within any 30s window. That yields roughly 270 concurrent active calls during peaks (customer-side estimate).
- Residency: US-only. SOC2 path okay; will request SOC2 + encryption attestation from procurement.

I've uploaded our short transcript logs to the Drive folder you shared; feel free to use those for more accurate token profiling.

Elena
Director, Engineering Ops, NovaPay
ops@novapay.com


From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-10T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: sizing numbers from NovaPay — asking for GPU recommendations and proforma inputs

Rafael / Marcus — forwarding Elena's confirmation above. Key constraints: 250 TPS peak, token mix ~65/30/5, ~270 concurrent active calls in peak window, p95 latency goals. Can you propose: 

- 2–3 dedicated GPU SKU options (e.g., H100-heavy vs A100 mixed vs L4 for short inference) with expected token/s GPU TPS and expected p95 latency for short vs long requests. Please include KV cache sizing assumptions and whether multi-node sharding or model variants (quantized) are recommended.
- Recommended batching/concurrency (tokens-in-flight) defaults we would set in the proforma to meet p95 SLA targets while minimizing cost.
- Any redlines on feasibility for 1.2s for long-context ops at 250 TPS peak.

I'll combine your inputs into the attached proforma and send back to Elena with cost/latency tradeoffs and timeline for Dedicated vs Hosted pilot.

Thanks,
Ava


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-10T13:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: sizing numbers from NovaPay — GPU recommendations

Quick pass from infra: high level guidance below; we can turn these into the proforma numbers once Marcus confirms cost model tweaks.

Option A — H100-focused (lowest latency for long context):
- Single H100 (80GB) perf note: ~8–11 short-completion TPS at 1/seq=140/resp=140 with aggressive kernel selection; with batching/prefetch we can push effective short-completion throughput closer to 18–20 TPS per GPU for highly quantized 8-bit models.
- For NovaPay peak (250 TPS short-heavy), estimate 12–16 H100s if we run mixed batching and reserve headroom for KV cache hot keys. Long requests (1,200 tokens) gravitate to 2–3x higher GPU time; plan for additional concurrency headroom (estimate +30% GPUs to keep p95 under 1.2s).
- KV/cache: dedicating ~200GB per pool for prefix/KV cache recommended (fast SSD-backed local cache across nodes).

Option B — A100-mix (cost / latency balance):
- A100 40GB: ~6–9 effective TPS for medium short requests, but denser packing and cheaper per-hour. For your mix, ~20 A100s gives predictable behavior and lower $/token than H100 option but slightly higher p95 on long runs.

Option C — Hybrid with L4 for short completions + H100 for long context:
- Offload >60% of short requests to L4 instances (cheaper), and reserve H100s for long-context and fallback. This reduces cost significantly but adds routing complexity and model-variant validation.

Batching/course defaults: 12–20ms target batching window for short completions; limit dynamic-batch size to keep p95 < 350ms. Recommend adaptive batching that tightens when queue latency approaches threshold.

Feasibility note: hitting 1.2s p95 for 1,200-token requests at 250 TPS is possible but expensive if all requests are long — our sizing assumes only ~5% long requests as you reported. If long-request % increases, GPU counts scale superlinearly.

I'll prepare precise GPU counts and cost delta for A/B variants (H100-only, A100 mix, hybrid) and drop into the proforma.

- Rafael
Senior Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-10T15:03:00-07:00

Adding pricing and procurement timeline notes for the proforma:

- Lead times: Dedicated pool provisioning (US region) ~4–6 weeks once contract signed (shorter if we can use reserved infra we have queued). Private VPC onboarding ~8–12 weeks (adds network review + KMS integration).
- Procurement ask: include SOC2 docs and an initial PO estimate to unblock infra procurement.

I'll update nova_capacity_proforma.xlsx with node counts, expected headroom, and per-node throughput assumptions and send a versioned copy back to you. Ava — once you get Rafael's final numbers, please attach the proforma and a DoR checklist for NovaPay procurement; we should ask for a soft commit on timeline in the reply.

- Peak TPS target: ~250 short-completion calls per second during batch settlement windows (peaks last ~10–15 min). Off-peak ~30 TPS.
- Token mix: average request ~220 input tokens, 140 output tokens; 95thpct requests up to 1,200 tokens (long receipts + context).
- Latency target: p95 < 350ms for short completions, p95 < 1.2s for long-context ops.
- Data residency: US-only; SOC2 evidence required.

2) Deployment pathways we discussed:
- Hosted API: fastest to production but shared pool; recommend only for non-sensitive early rollout.
- Dedicated capacity: reserved GPU pools (best predictability for your TPS bursts), supports KV/prefix caching to lower cost on repeated contexts.
- Private VPC/on-prem: for eventual regulated workflows; longer lead time and integration work.

3) Sizing asks we need from you to produce a proforma (we added a basic worksheet attached):
- Clarify percent of calls that are short (<400 tokens) vs long (>400 tokens) during peak.
- Any expected concurrency from your frontend (concurrent open sessions interacting with chat/generate API)?
- Confirmation if 99% of requests will be within US region.

Next steps: if you can confirm the short/long mix and typical concurrency by Friday, we'll return a dedicated-capacity proforma (GPU mix options + cost/latency tradeoffs) and a hosted fall-back plan.

Attachments: I attached the blank proforma and our SOC2 summary for procurement.

Cheers,
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

--
Ava Chen
Redwood Inference
ava@redwood.com
(415) 555-0192

Subject: Re: Post-call recap + proforma request (hosted vs dedicated vs private)

Hi Ava — thanks for the tidy recap. Confirming the numbers from our end: 

- Peak TPS window: 250 TPS (as discussed). We expect this to be concentrated in three 12–15 minute windows per day.
- Token mix estimate (rough): ~65% short (<=400 tokens), ~30% medium (401–800), ~5% long (>800).
- Average concurrency: frontend keeps ~1.8k concurrent open sessions during peak; estimate ~15% of those actively sending requests within any 30s window. That yields roughly 270 concurrent active calls during peaks (customer-side estimate).
- Residency: US-only. SOC2 path okay; will request SOC2 + encryption attestation from procurement.

Elena

Rafael / Marcus — forwarding Elena's confirmation above. Key constraints: 250 TPS peak, token mix ~65/30/5, ~270 concurrent active calls in peak window, p95 latency goals. Can you propose: 

- 2–3 dedicated GPU SKU options (e.g., H100-heavy vs A100 mixed vs L4 for short inference) with expected token/s GPU TPS and expected p95 latency for short vs long requests. Please include KV cache sizing assumptions and whether multi-node sharding or model variants (quantized) are recommended.
- Recommended batching/concurrency (tokens-in-flight) defaults we would set in the proforma to meet p95 SLA targets while minimizing cost.
- Any redlines on feasibility for 1.2s for long-context ops at 250 TPS peak.

I'll combine your inputs into the attached proforma and send back to Elena with cost/latency tradeoffs and timeline for Dedicated vs Hosted pilot.

Thanks,
Ava


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-06-10T13:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: sizing numbers from NovaPay — GPU recommendations

Quick pass from infra: high level guidance below; we can turn these into the proforma numbers once Marcus confirms cost model tweaks.

Option A — H100-focused (lowest latency for long context):
- Single H100 (80GB) perf note: ~8–11 short-completion TPS at 1/seq=140/resp=140 with aggressive kernel selection; with batching/prefetch we can push effective short-completion throughput closer to 18–20 TPS per GPU for highly quantized 8-bit models.
- For NovaPay peak (250 TPS short-heavy), estimate 12–16 H100s if we run mixed batching and reserve headroom for KV cache hot keys. Long requests (1,200 tokens) gravitate to 2–3x higher GPU time; plan for additional concurrency headroom (estimate +30% GPUs to keep p95 under 1.2s).
- KV/cache: dedicating ~200GB per pool for prefix/KV cache recommended (fast SSD-backed local cache across nodes).

Option B — A100-mix (cost / latency balance):
- A100 40GB: ~6–9 effective TPS for medium short requests, but denser packing and cheaper per-hour. For your mix, ~20 A100s gives predictable behavior and lower $/token than H100 option but slightly higher p95 on long runs.

Option C — Hybrid with L4 for short completions + H100 for long context:
- Offload >60% of short requests to L4 instances (cheaper), and reserve H100s for long-context and fallback. This reduces cost significantly but adds routing complexity and model-variant validation.

Batching/course defaults: 12–20ms target batching window for short completions; limit dynamic-batch size to keep p95 < 350ms. Recommend adaptive batching that tightens when queue latency approaches threshold.

Feasibility note: hitting 1.2s p95 for 1,200-token requests at 250 TPS is possible but expensive if all requests are long — our sizing assumes only ~5% long requests as you reported. If long-request % increases, GPU counts scale superlinearly.

I'll prepare precise GPU counts and cost delta for A/B variants (H100-only, A100 mix, hybrid) and drop into the proforma.

- Rafael
Senior Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference

title: Partner cost-sharing for vector-to-S3 eviction policy
preview:
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena@pinecone.io>, Marco Rossi <marco@weaviate.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 05 Jun 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Partner cost-sharing for vector-to-S3 eviction policy

Hi Elena / Marco —

Following up from last week's partner sync: we need to land on an operational and commercial model for how partner storage and cold spillover costs are allocated when customer traffic causes vector DB eviction to S3 (or S3 read-through) under hybrid index setups. This is especially important for customers in the Acme Retail pilot where we've seen frequent cold churn spikes during batch reindex windows.

Attached is a draft cost-split spreadsheet (cost-split-draft.xlsx) and a sketch of the spillover flow (diagram_spillover.drawio). High-level ask: can each partner review the proposed allocation logic and confirm whether you can: 1) emit the usage telemetry we need (eviction counts, bytes spilled, egress calls), and 2) accept a partner-facing invoice or credit flow based on the chosen model?

We proposed two models in the spreadsheet:
- Split by bytes spilled (simple)
- Split by query-weighted usage (favours partner if they serve more reads after cold warm-up)

If possible, please review by EOD Wednesday (2027-06-09). If there are immediate no-go items (e.g., egress billing constraints), flag them here and we'll schedule a short tech+commercial sync.

Thanks — appreciate a quick read.

Alex Martinez
Product Partnerships, Redwood Inference
alex.martinez@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0112

---
Attachment: cost-split-draft.xlsx (spreadsheet, 14KB)
Attachment: diagram_spillover.drawio (diagram, 8KB)

From: Elena Morales <elena@pinecone.io>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 05 Jun 2027 10:40:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Partner cost-sharing for vector-to-S3 eviction policy

Alex —

Thanks for the clear note and attachments. Pinecone can provide eviction and spill telemetry (evictions/sec, bytes_spilled/hour, per-index counters) but two quick concerns from our side:

1) Egress semantics: in many cloud setups egress from Pinecone to the customer's S3 (or between regions) can be billed at a different rate depending on the customer's cloud account. We need to define which account incurs egress and whether Redwood will aggregate and bill back.

2) Warm-up/anti-eviction incentives: if we surface a cost to partners purely by bytes spilled, that creates a tension where partners will try to keep fewer segments in memory to avoid share payments. We think a hybrid approach that includes a small warm-up credit (partner covers X% of first-byte egress, Redwood credits for prefetch requests) could align behaviour.

I've looped Marco from Weaviate to see if the same telemetry is practical on their side (cc'd). Also, can you share a sample 24-hour traffic slice for Acme Retail so we can model real churn?

Happy to jump on a 30m sync next week — propose Tue 6/8 11:00 PDT.

Best,
Elena Morales
Strategic Partnerships, Pinecone
elena@pinecone.io

---
(Quoted message)
On Fri, Jun 5, 2027 at 9:12 AM Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Elena / Marco —
> 
> Following up from last week's partner sync: we need to land on an operational and commercial model for how partner storage and cold spillover costs are allocated when customer traffic causes vector DB eviction to S3...


From: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena@pinecone.io>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 06 Jun 2027 08:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Partner cost-sharing for vector-to-S3 eviction policy — technical notes

Elena / Alex —

Quick engineering notes after skimming the draft:

- Eviction triggers: we typically see two dominant causes for spillover: large batch reindexing (bulk writes) and temporal access spikes on long-tail documents. Our metric of choice to attribute cost is "eviction-weighted-queries", which multiplies evicted_bytes by the number of subsequent queries that required rehydration within a 12-hour window.

- Cache TTLs & prefetch: if partners can expose an "estimated warm cache size" and allow Redwood to request proactive prefetches for hot keys, we can avoid a large class of rehydration reads. Prefetches should be flagged so they don't get double-counted as normal queries.

- Accounting parity: for fairness we need both sides to surface a) per-index eviction_count, b) per-index bytes_spilled, and c) rehydrate_reads_for_evicted_keys. Pinecone's approach to counts looks compatible based on Elena's note; Weaviate may need an SDK change.

- SLO impact: any commercial split must be paired with SLA language that ties predictable short-term latency degradation (during massive rehydration) to a remediation path (credits or temporary capacity uplift).

I attached a short note with heuristics (eviction-heuristics.md). Happy to present these metrics in the sync.

Soojin Lee
Engineering Manager, Indexing & Runtime, Redwood Inference
soojin.lee@redwood.com

---
Attachment: eviction-heuristics.md (text, 6KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>, Elena Morales <elena@pinecone.io>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 08 Jun 2027 11:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Partner cost-sharing for vector-to-S3 eviction policy — commercial proposal

Alex / Elena —

Commercially, for pilots (Acme Retail + two mid-market customers) I propose a floor/split approach to reduce churn on refresh:

- Floor: Redwood covers the first $0.10 per 10GB/day of spill (to absorb noise).
- Split: Remaining costs split 60/40 (Redwood/Partner) when rehydrate_reads_for_evicted_keys > 500 per index per day.
- Credit window: if the partner cannot meet the telemetry requirements, the partner agrees to a larger split (70/30) or provide credits to cover missing visibility.

Attached is a light term sheet capturing these numbers (commercial-term-sheet_v3.pdf). Key ask for partners: can you commit to surfacing the 3 metrics Soojin called out and accept an aggregated monthly billing reconciliation?

If Pinecone prefers an API-based billing callback instead of invoices, we can accept that — just need one of the flows.

Ben Carter

Subject: Partner cost-sharing for vector-to-S3 eviction policy

Hi Elena / Marco —

Following up from last week's partner sync: we need to land on an operational and commercial model for how partner storage and cold spillover costs are allocated when customer traffic causes vector DB eviction to S3 (or S3 read-through) under hybrid index setups. This is especially important for customers in the Acme Retail pilot where we've seen frequent cold churn spikes during batch reindex windows.

Attached is a draft cost-split spreadsheet (cost-split-draft.xlsx) and a sketch of the spillover flow (diagram_spillover.drawio). High-level ask: can each partner review the proposed allocation logic and confirm whether you can: 1) emit the usage telemetry we need (eviction counts, bytes spilled, egress calls), and 2) accept a partner-facing invoice or credit flow based on the chosen model?

We proposed two models in the spreadsheet:
- Split by bytes spilled (simple)
- Split by query-weighted usage (favours partner if they serve more reads after cold warm-up)

If possible, please review by EOD Wednesday (2027-06-09). If there are immediate no-go items (e.g., egress billing constraints), flag them here and we'll schedule a short tech+commercial sync.

Thanks — appreciate a quick read.

Alex Martinez
Product Partnerships, Redwood Inference
alex.martinez@redwood.com

From: Elena Morales <elena@pinecone.io>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 05 Jun 2027 10:40:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Partner cost-sharing for vector-to-S3 eviction policy

Alex —

Thanks for the clear note and attachments. Pinecone can provide eviction and spill telemetry (evictions/sec, bytes_spilled/hour, per-index counters) but two quick concerns from our side:

1) Egress semantics: in many cloud setups egress from Pinecone to the customer's S3 (or between regions) can be billed at a different rate depending on the customer's cloud account. We need to define which account incurs egress and whether Redwood will aggregate and bill back.

2) Warm-up/anti-eviction incentives: if we surface a cost to partners purely by bytes spilled, that creates a tension where partners will try to keep fewer segments in memory to avoid share payments. We think a hybrid approach that includes a small warm-up credit (partner covers X% of first-byte egress, Redwood credits for prefetch requests) could align behaviour.

I've looped Marco from Weaviate to see if the same telemetry is practical on their side (cc'd). Also, can you share a sample 24-hour traffic slice for Acme Retail so we can model real churn?

Happy to jump on a 30m sync next week — propose Tue 6/8 11:00 PDT.

Best,
Elena Morales
Strategic Partnerships, Pinecone

From: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena@pinecone.io>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 06 Jun 2027 08:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Partner cost-sharing for vector-to-S3 eviction policy — technical notes

Elena / Alex —

Quick engineering notes after skimming the draft:

- Eviction triggers: we typically see two dominant causes for spillover: large batch reindexing (bulk writes) and temporal access spikes on long-tail documents. Our metric of choice to attribute cost is "eviction-weighted-queries", which multiplies evicted_bytes by the number of subsequent queries that required rehydration within a 12-hour window.

- Cache TTLs & prefetch: if partners can expose an "estimated warm cache size" and allow Redwood to request proactive prefetches for hot keys, we can avoid a large class of rehydration reads. Prefetches should be flagged so they don't get double-counted as normal queries.

- Accounting parity: for fairness we need both sides to surface a) per-index eviction_count, b) per-index bytes_spilled, and c) rehydrate_reads_for_evicted_keys. Pinecone's approach to counts looks compatible based on Elena's note; Weaviate may need an SDK change.

- SLO impact: any commercial split must be paired with SLA language that ties predictable short-term latency degradation (during massive rehydration) to a remediation path (credits or temporary capacity uplift).

I attached a short note with heuristics (eviction-heuristics.md). Happy to present these metrics in the sync.

Soojin Lee
Engineering Manager, Indexing & Runtime, Redwood Inference
soojin.lee@redwood.com

title: Cross-cloud evaluation playbook + next steps (post-demo)
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Cc: Samantha Price <samantha.price@cloudforge.com>
Date: Mon, 7 Jun 2027 11:05:00 -0700
Subject: Cross-cloud evaluation playbook + next steps (post-demo)

Team —

Quick recap from this morning's call with CloudForge (Samantha) and a tentative action plan. High-level takeaways:

- CloudForge is running initial comparisons against two hyperscaler inference endpoints (HyperscaleA, HyperEdge) and saw p95 deltas that worry their infra team for real-time scoring (p95 ~210–290ms on HyperEdge vs ~110–140ms on Redwood-hosted variant in our demo). They want a reproducible A/B run.
- Primary ask: a concise A/B protocol they can run (or we run jointly) to measure p95/p99 and sustained tokens/sec at 256/1024/2048 sequence lengths.
- Secondary ask: cost-per-million-tokens estimate across the configurations and a short write-up on fallbacks (model variants + quantization) that preserve latency.

Proposed next steps (please action):

1) Benchmark playbook draft (Marcus): produce a short YAML/config that codifies runtime variants, concurrency, payload shapes, and success criteria (p95 < 200ms for interactive use; p99 < 350ms). Include cold-start and steady-state runs.

2) Test matrix (Jordan): pick the 3 windows for a joint run with CloudForge next week — we should offer at least two time slots in their PDT window. Include a canary run that uses our quantized-8bit variant and a baseline float16 run.

3) Data & artifacts (Ben): share the call recording and initial CSV they sent, plus a HubSpot link & folder for artifacts. I'll coordinate logistics with Arjun at Edgecraft if we need alternate infra.

Bench metrics to capture (quick reference):
- p50 / p95 / p99 latency (server-side)
- tokens/sec (steady-state with concurrency sweep)
- end-to-end wall time (network + server)
- cost estimate: CPU/GPU time translated to token unit economics

Attachments: CF-call-recording.mp4 (recording), cloudforge-initial-metrics.csv (their quick probe).

Samantha — thanks again for the time. Jordan will send calendar options; Marcus will post a config by EOD tomorrow. If there are additional measurements your SRE wants, drop them in the thread and we'll fold them into the playbook.

Ben
Ben Carter | Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0123
https://redwood.ai

---

[Attachment: CF-call-recording.mp4, cloudforge-initial-metrics.csv]

title: FastCart signup — demo & qualification
preview:
From: Samir Patel <samir@fastcart.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Rao <priya@fastcart.ai>
Date: Tue, 8 Jun 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: FastCart signup — quick demo?

Hey Alex,

We just signed up for the self-serve tier and started poking at the API. FastCart is an ecommerce search + discovery startup — primary use case is product search relevance + a conversational shopping assistant on product pages.

We have a few concrete asks before we schedule a demo:
- Expected sandbox volume: ~500k embeddings/month (initial).
- Generation: approx 200k tokens/day during peak tests; QPS ramp to ~20 req/s in pilot.
- Timeline: we'd like a short pilot in the next 6–8 weeks and evaluate Dedicated/Private options later.
- Security: SOC 2 evidence + SAML SSO for console users.

Can you share an engineer-led demo and a quick checklist of what you need from us (test data, sample prompts, network access)? I'm in PST — available Tue/Wed afternoons.

Thanks,
Samir
Samir Patel
Head of ML, FastCart

Subject: Demo booking — Redwood Inference (time looks off)

title: Next steps: embedding pipeline decision log & function-call/streaming approach
preview:
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Moreno <alex.moreno@novaly.io>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 07 Jun 2027 14:05:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Discovery — embeddings + rerank + streaming funcalls (post-call recap)

Alex — thanks again for taking the call today. Quick recap while it's fresh and a proposed set of decisions so we can lock an implementation path. TL;DR: you want a deterministic rerank/embedding pipeline for patient record retrieval, predictable latency for 95th pct, and a lightweight function-calling surface for structured output to downstream EHR processors.

Key pain points you mentioned:
- Search recall variability across long clinical notes (embeddings drift across updates).
- Token-level cost spikes during peak triage windows (evening PST).
- Need for a single, auditable structured output schema for downstream ingestion into your CDW.

Goals we aligned on:
1) Establish an embedding refresh cadence and indexing strategy that keeps recall stable while bounding costs.
2) Serve reranking at sub-200ms p95 for top-5 candidates for triage paths.
3) Deliver a function-call interface that returns validated JSON records (schema v1) for automated ingestion + an opt-in streaming path for long responses.

Proposed technical decisions (please flag anything you disagree with):
A) Embedding pipeline: incremental embedding updates every 6 hours for active patients, nightly full reindex for historical backlog. Use 1536-d quantized embeddings (recommended model) with similarity index sharding by facility.
B) Reranker: vector search + lightweight cross-encoder on top for top-20 -> top-5 narrowing; deploy on Dedicated capacity to meet p95 SLAs.
C) Function-calling: Redwood SDK function schema enforcement (we'll commit schema v1 and autoparse failures to a dead-letter queue). Include a small set of pre-defined transforms for Novaly (patient_summary, triage_score, evidence_pointers).
D) Streaming: optional streaming output for long narrative generation; default is non-streaming for triage paths to preserve determinism.

Next steps I propose:
- Ben to send a short mutual action plan (MAP) with dates and owners (EOD tomorrow).
- Novaly to confirm schema v1 fields and any PII redaction rules by EOD Thu.
- We will prepare a small SDK patch (diff) showing the function-call validation hooks and a sample streaming toggle.

Attachments: will share MAP and sdk change proposal in the follow-up.

Thanks — Jordan
Jordan Blake
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
jordan.blake@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0199



From: Alex Moreno <alex.moreno@novaly.io>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Cc: Novaly CTO Team <cto@novaly.io>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 07 Jun 2027 16:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Discovery — embeddings + rerank + streaming funcalls (post-call recap)

Jordan — great summary and thanks. A couple of clarifications from our side: we can commit to delivering the schema v1 draft by Thursday, but we need to keep a conservative set of fields initially (patient_id, encounter_id, triage_level, summary_snippet, evidence_refs).

On the embedding cadence: 6-hour incremental looks reasonable for active patients. Two constraints: (1) our busiest facilities are in CST/EST so evening peaks are different, and (2) we need a way to mark records as sensitive so they are excluded from embeddings (PHI subset).

About latency: sub-200ms p95 for top-5 is ambitious but necessary. If Dedicated capacity is the approach, can you give a ballpark for the committed GPU profile and expected cost delta vs hosted per-token? We need that to build our business case.

Finally, quick ask — can we see a minimal example of the function-call JSON you mentioned (patient_summary) so our ingestion team can validate?

Thanks — Alex
Alex Moreno
CTO, Novaly Health
alex.moreno@novaly.io | +1 617-555-0123

On Mon, Jun 07, 2027 at 14:05 Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Alex — thanks again for taking the call today. Quick recap while it's fresh...



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Cc: Alex Moreno <alex.moreno@novaly.io>
Date: Tue, 08 Jun 2027 08:10:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Discovery — embeddings + rerank + streaming funcalls (post-call recap)

Jordan / Alex — I attached the MAP draft and a short SDK patch that illustrates the function-call validation hook and streaming toggle. High-level answers to Alex's questions: 

1) Dedicated profile: estimate is 4x A10G-equivalent GPUs (sharded) for a predictable p95 under 200ms for top-5 across the triage workload we saw in your sample traces. Cost delta vs hosted is roughly a 1.7x uplift for steady-state (discounted further under an annual commit). I'll surface a detailed cost sheet once we have expected QPS.

2) Sensitive-record exclusion: we'll add tag-based filters in the pipeline. Novaly should provide a list of sensitivity tags and mapping rules (attached MAP has a placeholder).

3) Function-call sample (inline below) — this is the patient_summary response schema we propose in schema v1:
{
  \"patient_id\": \"string\",
  \"encounter_id\": \"string\",
  \"triage_level\": \"low|moderate|high\",
  \"summary_snippet\": \"string\",
  \"evidence_refs\": [\"ref_id1\", \"ref_id2\"]
}

Notes on streaming: SDK exposes a streaming=true flag — when set, Redwood will emit partial JSON events with a final commit event. For deterministic triage, keep streaming=false; for clinician-facing narrative, set streaming=true.

I'll update the MAP with owners and dates (Jordan, can you include expected QPS estimate from Novaly?).

-ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai

Attachments: MAP_Novaly_20270607.pdf, sdk_change_proposal.patch



From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Moreno <alex.moreno@novaly.io>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 08 Jun 2027 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: Finalizing MAP & immediate asks (Novaly)

Alex — following Ben's notes, attaching the MAP and patch. Final, explicit asks so we can hit milestones next week:
1) Novaly: provide expected QPS (p95/mean) for triage queries and a list of sensitivity tags by EOD Friday (2027-06-11).
2) Novaly: confirm you can produce schema v1 draft (fields above) by Thu 2027-06-10 so we can implement validation in the SDK patch.
3) Redwood: Ben and I will circulate a cost appendix once we get QPS; we will also prepare a short performance test plan for a dedicated pilot.

If that works, Ben will schedule a 45-minute alignment next Tue to walk the Novaly engineers through the SDK change. I can do a short demo of streaming vs non-streaming behavior during that session.

Regards — Jordan

[Attachment: MAP_Novaly_20270607.pdf]
[Attachment: sdk_change_proposal.patch]

title: Sierra Analytics — sizing ask: committed TPS, latency headroom, and fallback
preview:
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwoodinference.com>
To: Melissa Cheng <melissa@hypersight.ai>
Date: Sat, 05 Jun 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: initial sizing numbers for Dedicated commit

Hi Melissa — good to connect last week. Quick follow-up: to build a committed Dedicated proposal and committed capacity pricing for Sierra, can you confirm the following items and share any attachment you have for traffic profiles?

1) Expected 95th-percentile sustained tokens/sec during peak windows (per-organization) and typical burst multiplier (e.g., 3x short bursts).
2) Average request generation length (tokens) and max expected generation length.
3) Percent of requests that are chat (multi-turn) vs single-prompt inference.
4) Required latency SLOs (p95 and p99) for interactive flows; batch/async tolerances if any.
5) Any regulatory/regional restrictions for data residency.

If you can attach a CSV or a simple spreadsheet that shows hourly TPS/tokens for a week, that would be ideal — we use that to simulate batching/caching effects in our latency model.

I’ve copied Ben (capacity), Sergio (runtime optimizations), and Lauren (commercial) so we can run a quick runbook after we get the numbers.

Thanks — Aisha

--
Aisha Rahman
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
[aisha@redwoodinference.com]
[Attachment: request-checklist.pdf (stub)]

From: Melissa Cheng <melissa@hypersight.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement@sierranet.com
Date: Mon, 07 Jun 2027 08:23:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: initial sizing numbers for Dedicated commit

Hi Aisha — thanks. Attached is the demand profile you asked for. High-level callouts:

- Peak sustained tokens/sec (95th): 45,000 tokens/sec across the tenant during our top 2-hour window (we can provide per-route breakout if helpful).
- Burst behavior: short spikes up to ~140,000 tokens/sec observed (3.1x).
- Avg generation length: 80 tokens; max observed 1,200 tokens (rare).
- Chat vs single-turn: ~68% chat (multi-turn). KV cache hit estimated at ~22% (session affinity).
- Latency requirements: interactive product flows p95 <= 250 ms (including network), p99 <= 450 ms. Background batch jobs can be 2–5s.
- Residency: US only for now.

Attachments: sierra_demand_profile.csv (hourly tokens and route), SLA-reqs.pdf.

Let us know the earliest you can run a model and share a recommended committed slab + fallback.

Best,
Melissa
Head of Product, Sierra Analytics
[Attachment: sierra_demand_profile.csv (CSV, 48KB)]

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwoodinference.com>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio_alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 07 Jun 2027 10:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Sierra demand profile + sizing ask — need throughput/latency model

Forwarding Sierra's demand profile — please see Melissa's note below. Quick ask: can we produce a deal-specific throughput/latency model (tokens/sec estimates and SLO fit) and a recommended Dedicated slab configuration with headroom and fallback policies? Key constraints:

- Model they plan to run: redwood-open-7b-q4 (producer says 7B-family, quantized). We should validate perf on our A100-80GB baseline; if we need other shapes call it out.
- Peak 95th sustained: 45k tokens/sec; bursts up to 140k. Average gen length 80 tokens, chat-heavy.
- Latency SLO target: p95 <= 250ms (end-to-end).

Please include in the output:
- Estimated tokens/sec per GPU (A100-80GB) for their mix and sequence length distribution — show the math.
- Required number of GPUs for committed slab to meet p95 with a safety multiplier (recommendation + rationale).
- Burst/overflow plan (fallback to hosted + model variant quantization, warm KV cache strategy).
- Ops runbook notes: cold-start cache warming, autoscale thresholds, target queue latency.

Ben — can you run the batch of perf numbers and produce the initial Excel model? Sergio — please flag any runtime knobs (KV cache hit ops, quantization adjustments) that would materially reduce GPU needs. Lauren — I'll need a commercial blurb and suggested commitment term and price uplift for headroom.

Deadline: can we have a first-pass by EOD tomorrow PST?

Thanks — Aisha

--
Aisha Rahman
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
[Attachment: latency-model-v2.xlsx (stub)]

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwoodinference.com>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio_alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 08 Jun 2027 16:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Sierra demand profile + sizing ask — recommended slab and headroom

Team — ran a quick model (attached) and summarized below. Assumptions and rationale first, then recommended committed slab.

Assumptions (baseline):
- Baseline serving config: redwood-open-7b-q4 on A100-80GB nodes, single model instance per GPU with dynamic KV caching.
- Measured median tokens/sec per GPU (our internal microbench for 80-token average, multi-turn mix): ~3,800 tokens/sec sustained per A100-80GB for the observed chat-heavy mix (this includes inference + kv cache overhead).
- For longer sequences (avg 80 -> p95 includes occasional longer tails), effective per-GPU sustained drops to ~3,200 tps when accounting for 10% extra model context processing and network overhead.
- Burst handling: we plan for 2.5x burst absorb capacity via pre-warmed host fallback pools + autoscale. Pure committed slab should be sized for sustained 95th load with a safety multiplier of 1.25x for ops variance.

Simple math (sustained):
- Required sustained GPUs = ceil(45,000 tokens/sec / 3,200 tokens/sec_per_gpu) = ceil(14.06) => 15 GPUs to cover 95th sustained without safety buffer.
- With safety multiplier 1.25 => 15 * 1.25 = 18.75 => recommend reserving 19 A100-80GB GPUs for committed slab.

Burst plan (140k observed):
- Committed slab (19 GPUs) provides ~60.8k tokens/sec sustained (19 * 3,200).
- For bursts to 140k, rely on a) warm hosted fallback pool sized to absorb remaining ~79k tokens/sec; b) autoscale burst nodes (ephemeral) and prioritized queueing for non-critical routes. Expect short p95 degradations during longest tails; recommend SLA language for burst-mode latency tolerance.

Latency fit:
- With 19 GPUs and KV cache warm, we expect p95 model-inference latency inside 160–190 ms; network/egress budget 40–90 ms depending on region/peering. Combined end-to-end p95 estimate ~220–280 ms. To meet strict 250 ms p95 target across all edge cases, we suggest:
  - Keep 19 GPUs committed, and maintain a small warm standby of 3 extra GPUs (hot-warm) capable of absorbing stateful sessions immediately. This pushes committed+standby to 22 GPUs and gives extra headroom for p95.

Ops notes / runbook highlights:
- Warm KV cache policy: pre-warm KV for top 20% of active session IDs (sourced from their session telemetry) during peak windows; this can recover ~12–18% effective tokens/sec improvement.
- Autoscale thresholds: trigger ephemeral scale when queue latency > 60ms for > 30s. Use queue-first throttling for non-interactive routes.
- Model fallback: offer a slightly faster quantized variant (redwood-open-7b-q4-fast) for degraded tail conditions; quality tests show ~2% NLL degradation but 20–25% higher tokens/sec.

Commercial suggestion (Lauren): recommend 12-month committed slab of 19 GPUs with ability to burst to hosted capacity. Price uplift for standby 3 GPUs included in an enhanced SLA tier. Suggest proposal: 19 committed GPUs + 3 hot-standby for guaranteed p95 <=250ms, with defined burst fallback to hosted at defined ingress rates.

Attachments: latency-model-v2.xlsx (attached with per-hour sims).

If you want I can run a more granular per-route simulation (per-route qps and session affinity) tomorrow — that will likely move the GPU requirement by +/-2 GPUs depending on cache hit optimizations.

Ben
Director, Capacity Planning, Redwood Inference
[Attachment: latency-model-v2.xlsx (XLSX, 76KB)]

To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwoodinference.com>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio_alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 07 Jun 2027 10:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Sierra demand profile + sizing ask — need throughput/latency model

Forwarding Sierra's demand profile — please see Melissa's note below. Quick ask: can we produce a deal-specific throughput/latency model (tokens/sec estimates and SLO fit) and a recommended Dedicated slab configuration with headroom and fallback policies? Key constraints:

- Model they plan to run: redwood-open-7b-q4 (producer says 7B-family, quantized). We should validate perf on our A100-80GB baseline; if we need other shapes call it out.
- Peak 95th sustained: 45k tokens/sec; bursts up to 140k. Average gen length 80 tokens, chat-heavy.
- Latency SLO target: p95 <= 250ms (end-to-end).

Please include in the output:
- Estimated tokens/sec per GPU (A100-80GB) for their mix and sequence length distribution — show the math.
- Required number of GPUs for committed slab to meet p95 with a safety multiplier (recommendation + rationale).
- Burst/overflow plan (fallback to hosted + model variant quantization, warm KV cache strategy).
- Ops runbook notes: cold-start cache warming, autoscale thresholds, target queue latency.

Ben — can you run the batch of perf numbers and produce the initial Excel model? Sergio — please flag any runtime knobs (KV cache hit ops, quantization adjustments) that would materially reduce GPU needs. Lauren — I'll need a commercial blurb and suggested commitment term and price uplift for headroom.

Deadline: can we have a first-pass by EOD tomorrow PST?

Thanks — Aisha

--
Aisha Rahman
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
[Attachment: latency-model-v2.xlsx (stub)]

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwoodinference.com>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio_alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 08 Jun 2027 16:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Sierra demand profile + sizing ask — recommended slab and headroom

Team — ran a quick model (attached) and summarized below. Assumptions and rationale first, then recommended committed slab.

Assumptions (baseline):
- Baseline serving config: redwood-open-7b-q4 on A100-80GB nodes, single model instance per GPU with dynamic KV caching.
- Measured median tokens/sec per GPU (our internal microbench for 80-token average, multi-turn mix): ~3,800 tokens/sec sustained per A100-80GB for the observed chat-heavy mix (this includes inference + kv cache overhead).
- For longer sequences (avg 80 -> p95 includes occasional longer tails), effective per-GPU sustained drops to ~3,200 tps when accounting for 10% extra model context processing and network overhead.
- Burst handling: we plan for 2.5x burst absorb capacity via pre-warmed host fallback pools + autoscale. Pure committed slab should be sized for sustained 95th load with a safety multiplier of 1.25x for ops variance.

Simple math (sustained):
- Required sustained GPUs = ceil(45,000 tokens/sec / 3,200 tokens/sec_per_gpu) = ceil(14.06) => 15 GPUs to cover 95th sustained without safety buffer.
- With safety multiplier 1.25 => 15 * 1.25 = 18.75 => recommend reserving 19 A100-80GB GPUs for committed slab.

Burst plan (140k observed):
- Committed slab (19 GPUs) provides ~60.8k tokens/sec sustained (19 * 3,200).
- For bursts to 140k, rely on a) warm hosted fallback pool sized to absorb remaining ~79k tokens/sec; b) autoscale burst nodes (ephemeral) and prioritized queueing for non-critical routes. Expect short p95 degradations during longest tails; recommend SLA language for burst-mode latency tolerance.

Latency fit:
- With 19 GPUs and KV cache warm, we expect p95 model-inference latency inside 160–190 ms; network/egress budget 40–90 ms depending on region/peering. Combined end-to-end p95 estimate ~220–280 ms. To meet strict 250 ms p95 target across all edge cases, we suggest:
  - Keep 19 GPUs committed, and maintain a small warm standby of 3 extra GPUs (hot-warm) capable of absorbing stateful sessions immediately. This pushes committed+standby to 22 GPUs and gives extra headroom for p95.

Ops notes / runbook highlights:
- Warm KV cache policy: pre-warm KV for top 20% of active session IDs (sourced from their session telemetry) during peak windows; this can recover ~12–18% effective tokens/sec improvement.
- Autoscale thresholds: trigger ephemeral scale when queue latency > 60ms for > 30s. Use queue-first throttling for non-interactive routes.
- Model fallback: offer a slightly faster quantized variant (redwood-open-7b-q4-fast) for degraded tail conditions; quality tests show ~2% NLL degradation but 20–25% higher tokens/sec.

Commercial suggestion (Lauren): recommend 12-month committed slab of 19 GPUs with ability to burst to hosted capacity. Price uplift for standby 3 GPUs included in an enhanced SLA tier. Suggest proposal: 19 committed GPUs + 3 hot-standby for guaranteed p95 <=250ms, with defined burst fallback to hosted at defined ingress rates.

Attachments: latency-model-v2.xlsx (attached with per-hour sims).

If you want I can run a more granular per-route simulation (per-route qps and session affinity) tomorrow — that will likely move the GPU requirement by +/-2 GPUs depending on cache hit optimizations.

Ben
Director, Capacity Planning, Redwood Inference
[Attachment: latency-model-v2.xlsx (XLSX, 76KB)]

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Cc: 
Date: Sun, 6 Jun 2027 10:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: RFP: Implementation & Migration Questionnaire - NovaCorp (draft responses)

Ben / Karthik —

Attached is a draft program response (ben-20270606-novacorp-impl-draft.docx). Highlights below so you can review quickly:

- Executive summary: 8-week engagement for pilot + production roll (60-day pilot included). We propose a 3-phase plan: 1) Onboard & validate (weeks 0–2), 2) Integration & pilot (weeks 3–6), 3) Production ramp & optimization (weeks 7–8+).
- Onboarding (weeks 0–2): API keys, VPC peering or private endpoint setup, test dataset handoff, integration of auth and logging. Required NovaCorp deliverables: network ACLs, a staging dataset subset, contact for incident ops.
- Integration & pilot (weeks 3–6): Implement SDK, map request flows, load test shadow traffic, seed cache if applicable. Pilot duration: 60 days with weekly checkpoint calls.
- Canary ramp (production cutover): staged traffic percentages — day 0: 1% (smoke), day 1–2: 5%, day 3–7: 20%, weeks 2–4: 50%–80% depending on callouts. Each step gated by metrics (error rate <0.5%, p95 latency < target, degradation in quality metrics within acceptable delta).
- Rollback & runbook: automatic rollback on persistent error spike (error rate >1% for 15m) or if quality regression is detected in A/B test window. Manual rollback steps included (DNS/traffic reverse, feature flag off, model fallback).
- Acceptance criteria for pilot: stable latency within SLA band, no P0 incidents for 14 consecutive days, and model quality checks (prompt set pass rate >= 95%).

Open items for NovaCorp: required IP CIDR ranges for whitelisting, preferred start date for canary window (we suggested 6/21 but need confirmation), and whether they need HSM/KMS integration for key material.

Attachment: ben-20270606-novacorp-impl-draft.docx

I recommend we include a short appendix on observability (what we will instrument: per-route token-level cost, p95/p99 latencies, model-level output drift alerts) — that’s in the draft.

—Marissa
Marissa Cole
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.ai


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: 
Date: Sun, 6 Jun 2027 14:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: RFP: Implementation & Migration Questionnaire - NovaCorp (technical notes)

Quick technical notes to include in the response:

1) Network & connectivity
- Support for private endpoint (VPC endpoint) or peering; recommend Private Endpoint for least blast radius. Minimum ports: 443 outbound. We need NovaCorp to provide the egress CIDR block to whitelist.
- For dedicated/private deployments requiring on-prem appliance, include a separate hardware onboarding checklist — not needed for hosted pilot.

2) Authentication & keys
- Use short-lived JWTs for application-level auth; for customer-managed keys we support KMS / HSM integration. If they require envelope encryption of payloads, we’ll coordinate key exchange and rotation cadence.

3) Migration seeding & cache
- If NovaCorp’s workload benefits from KV/prefix caching, we can seed prefix cache during pilot to reduce latency on first golden calls. Requires sanitized sample dataset.

4) Canary & failover controls
- Canary will be implemented at the routing layer with gradual traffic split and active health checks. Rollback triggers: sustained error rate >1% for 15 minutes OR increase in regression metric (prompt set failure) by >3% vs baseline.
- Fallback: automatic switch to a lower-cost/older model variant if newer model exhibits quality regression.

5) Instrumentation
- We will deliver per-route metrics (p50/p95/p99 latencies), token counters, and sampling of outputs for quality evals. Logs delivered to their syslog/S3 per agreement.

Attachment: network-diagram.png (diagram of recommended networking)

If they want a tighter canary (faster ramp), we need extra SLAs (standby capacity) which may affect pricing; happy to outline options.

—Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Tech Lead, Inference Platform
karthik.iyer@redwood.ai


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: John Sato <john.sato@novacorp.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 7 Jun 2027 08:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: RFP: Implementation & Migration Questionnaire - NovaCorp (Redwood response)

John —

Thanks for the questionnaire. Please find attached our consolidated implementation response and proposed milestone schedule. Below is an executive summary and the key asks from NovaCorp so you can circulate to your procurement/engineering teams quickly.

Executive summary:
- Proposed engagement: 8 weeks (onboarding + 60-day pilot). Target start: week of 2027-06-21.
- Pilot acceptance: pass the acceptance criteria below (latency, error rate, quality checks).
- Canary approach: 1% smoke → 5% validation → 20% short window → 50%+ ramp with gates.

Key NovaCorp deliverables required before start:
- Network egress CIDR for whitelisting + decision on private endpoint vs peering.
- Staging dataset (sanitized) for cache seeding and quality validation.
- Primary/secondary incident contacts and a nominated product owner for weekly check-ins.

Pilot acceptance criteria (summary):
- P95 latency within agreed SLA for 14 consecutive days during pilot windows.
- Error rate <0.5% sustained; no P0 incidents for 14 consecutive days.
- Model quality checks: prompt-set pass rate >=95% and no material regression versus baseline.

Runbook & rollback highlights:
- Automatic rollback on sustained error spike (>1% for 15 minutes) or model-quality regression >3% vs baseline.
- Manual rollback steps documented (traffic flip, model pin, DNS rollback) and we will rehearse failover during the pilot.

Attachments: Redwood_Response_NovaCorp_Implementation.pdf (formal responses, timeline, and runbook)

Next steps: if this aligns, can we schedule a 45-minute technical alignment call the week of 6/14 to confirm network and keys? I’ll also include a DocuSign link for the SOW draft once we confirm start window.

Best,

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>

Cc: 
Date: Sun, 6 Jun 2027 10:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: RFP: Implementation & Migration Questionnaire - NovaCorp (draft responses)

Ben / Karthik —

Attached is a draft program response (ben-20270606-novacorp-impl-draft.docx). Highlights below so you can review quickly:

- Executive summary: 8-week engagement for pilot + production roll (60-day pilot included). We propose a 3-phase plan: 1) Onboard & validate (weeks 0–2), 2) Integration & pilot (weeks 3–6), 3) Production ramp & optimization (weeks 7–8+).
- Onboarding (weeks 0–2): API keys, VPC peering or private endpoint setup, test dataset handoff, integration of auth and logging. Required NovaCorp deliverables: network ACLs, a staging dataset subset, contact for incident ops.
- Integration & pilot (weeks 3–6): Implement SDK, map request flows, load test shadow traffic, seed cache if applicable. Pilot duration: 60 days with weekly checkpoint calls.
- Canary ramp (production cutover): staged traffic percentages — day 0: 1% (smoke), day 1–2: 5%, day 3–7: 20%, weeks 2–4: 50%–80% depending on callouts. Each step gated by metrics (error rate <0.5%, p95 latency < target, degradation in quality metrics within acceptable delta).
- Rollback & runbook: automatic rollback on persistent error spike (error rate >1% for 15m) or if quality regression is detected in A/B test window. Manual rollback steps included (DNS/traffic reverse, feature flag off, model fallback).
- Acceptance criteria for pilot: stable latency within SLA band, no P0 incidents for 14 consecutive days, and model quality checks (prompt set pass rate >= 95%).

Open items for NovaCorp: required IP CIDR ranges for whitelisting, preferred start date for canary window (we suggested 6/21 but need confirmation), and whether they need HSM/KMS integration for key material.

Attachment: ben-20270606-novacorp-impl-draft.docx

I recommend we include a short appendix on observability (what we will instrument: per-route token-level cost, p95/p99 latencies, model-level output drift alerts) — that’s in the draft.

—Marissa
Marissa Cole
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.ai


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: 
Date: Sun, 6 Jun 2027 14:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: RFP: Implementation & Migration Questionnaire - NovaCorp (technical notes)

Quick technical notes to include in the response:

1) Network & connectivity
- Support for private endpoint (VPC endpoint) or peering; recommend Private Endpoint for least blast radius. Minimum ports: 443 outbound. We need NovaCorp to provide the egress CIDR block to whitelist.
- For dedicated/private deployments requiring on-prem appliance, include a separate hardware onboarding checklist — not needed for hosted pilot.

2) Authentication & keys
- Use short-lived JWTs for application-level auth; for customer-managed keys we support KMS / HSM integration. If they require envelope encryption of payloads, we’ll coordinate key exchange and rotation cadence.

3) Migration seeding & cache
- If NovaCorp’s workload benefits from KV/prefix caching, we can seed prefix cache during pilot to reduce latency on first golden calls. Requires sanitized sample dataset.

4) Canary & failover controls
- Canary will be implemented at the routing layer with gradual traffic split and active health checks. Rollback triggers: sustained error rate >1% for 15 minutes OR increase in regression metric (prompt set failure) by >3% vs baseline.
- Fallback: automatic switch to a lower-cost/older model variant if newer model exhibits quality regression.

5) Instrumentation
- We will deliver per-route metrics (p50/p95/p99 latencies), token counters, and sampling of outputs for quality evals. Logs delivered to their syslog/S3 per agreement.

Attachment: network-diagram.png (diagram of recommended networking)

If they want a tighter canary (faster ramp), we need extra SLAs (standby capacity) which may affect pricing; happy to outline options.

—Karthik

title: Proposed redlines — Term / Auto-renew / Termination for Convenience
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@acme.com>, procurement <procurement@acme.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 7 Jun 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Proposed redlines — Term, Auto‑renew & TFC

Hi Samantha / ACME procurement team,

Thanks for the productive demo last week. Attaching a first-pass redline to the MSA focused only on term/renewal/termination mechanics so legal can move quickly. High-level asks / notes from our side:

- Auto‑renew: propose mutual auto‑renew with a 45‑day opt‑out window prior to renewal (default is 12‑month renewal).
- Notice window: if ACME requests 60 days we can accept for enterprise deals > $500k; for pilots / < $250k we prefer 30–45 days.
- Termination for Convenience (TFC): we are open to a limited TFC but need a clear scope — propose TFC subject to 60 days' notice and pro‑rated refund for any prepaid amounts; non‑refundable committed fees (if any) should be limited to the final invoice month.
- Service continuity: add explicit handover obligations in an exit schedule (SOW-level tasks).

Please review the attached redline and confirm where ACME would like to be more aggressive. If you prefer, we can provide suggested language for the notice window and TFC carveouts so legal has a clean starting point.

Attachments: ACME_MSA_v2_redline.docx (redline)
HubSpot: https://hubspot.example/deal/3456

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com


From: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@acme.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: legal <legal@acme.com>, procurement <procurement@acme.com>
Date: Mon, 7 Jun 2027 11:00:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Proposed redlines — Term, Auto‑renew & TFC

Thanks Ben — appreciate the focused redline. A few initial points from procurement and legal that will drive our counter: 

- Notice window: Procurement prefers a 30‑day opt‑out for agreements under $250k, 60 days for larger enterprise deals. Can you confirm Redwood can accept that split?
- TFC: Legal is uncomfortable with open‑ended refunds. Their preference is (i) 30‑day cure, then (ii) pro‑rated refund for prepaid services, and (iii) a cap on Redwood's refund obligation (e.g., not more than remaining 6 months of committed fees) rather than unlimited exposure.
- Auto‑renew: ACME would like auto‑renew to default to month‑to‑month after the initial term for pilots; for enterprise we expect a 12‑month renewal term.

I've attached our procurement comments for visibility. If Redwood can propose specific alternative language for the notice tiers and a capped TFC refund, we can get legal to review faster.

Attachments: ACME_procurement_comments.docx

> 
> Thanks for the productive demo last week. Attaching a first‑pass redline to the MSA focused only on term/renewal/termination mechanics so legal can move quickly. High‑level asks / notes from our side: ...

samantha.lee@acme.com


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@acme.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, legal <legal@acme.com>
Date: Mon, 7 Jun 2027 13:24:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Proposed redlines — Term, Auto‑renew & TFC

Samantha — thanks for the quick reply and clear direction. We're comfortable with a split notice window (30 days for pilots/<$250k, 60 days for enterprise). Proposed compromises below you can circulate to legal as clean language: 

- Notice window: \"Customer or Redwood may provide notice of non‑renewal no fewer than 30 days prior to the renewal date for agreements with annualized committed spend below $250,000; for agreements with annualized committed spend equal to or above $250,000, notice must be provided no fewer than 60 days prior to the renewal date.\"

- TFC: \"Either party may terminate this Agreement for convenience upon sixty (60) days' prior written notice. In the event of termination for convenience by Customer, Customer shall be entitled to a pro‑rated refund of prepaid, unused fees for services not yet delivered, subject to a cap equal to six (6) months of Customer's average monthly invoice during the twelve (12) months preceding termination. Redwood's liability under this Section shall be limited to the refund of prepaid fees; nothing herein shall be construed to require Redwood to reimburse costs associated with third‑party marketplace fees or hardware lease obligations.\"

- Auto‑renew: For pilot commitments, we propose an option in the Order Form to set renewal to month‑to‑month after the initial term; for enterprise we keep a 12 month renewal by default.

We'll attach a short term sheet to make the tradeoffs explicit for your procurement/legal teams. If that language looks reasonable, happy to move to redline the MSA with these concrete edits so your counsel has a version to approve.

Attachments: Redwood_Term_Sheet_20270607.pdf

Thanks,
Ben

preview:
From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>

Team —

Thanks for the quick turnaround on yesterday's \"Scaling Inference\" webinar. Attached are the three artifacts you asked for: attendee export, our prioritized hot list (MQLs -> PQLs flagged), and the replay packet with slide timestamps and key quote clips. High-level guidance below so SDRs can act today: 

- Hot list: 48 accounts flagged as \"high\" (column: priority=\"H\"). Focus on accounts marked with a green check in the \"on-platform\" column (we already have cloud entitlement data).
- Recommended action by priority:
  * H: Outreach + 30-minute discovery (offer demo window)
  * M: Nurture touch, send focused snippet from the replay + content pack
  * L: Add to quarterly nurture list
- Suggested demo SLO: book 40% of H list within 7 business days.
- Routing: regional owners first, then overflow queue (see mapping in the sheet).

Replay link (share with attendees only): https://redwoodinference.com/replay/20270604-enterprise-econ
Drive folder with assets: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3c-redwood-webinar-20270604

Attachments:
- attendee_export_20270604.csv (email, company, role, event engagement score)
- hotlist_priority_20270604.xlsx (routing, priority, notes)
- replay_packet_20270604.pdf (clips + suggested outreach snippets)

Next steps / asks:
- Aditya: please confirm SDR claim plan for H accounts and how you'll split overflow.
- Ben: take the west-coast H list and start booking.
- Marissa: push replay emails to attendees who registered but didn't watch (use snippets from the packet).

I'll be available for clarifying any lead signals.

Lauren Bishop
Head of Events, Redwood Inference
lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com

To: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2027-06-05 10:12 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-webinar package: attendee export, hotlist, replay link

Thanks Lauren — this is perfect. Confirming plan and adding a few operational details so SDRs have clear acceptance criteria. Quoting your action items below for context.

On 2027-06-05 09:05, Lauren Bishop wrote:
> Team —
> 
> Thanks for the quick turnaround on yesterday's \"Scaling Inference\" webinar. Attached are the three artifacts you asked for: attendee export, our prioritized hot list (MQLs -> PQLs flagged), and the replay packet with slide timestamps and key quote clips....

My plan: 
- I'll carve the H list into two buckets: West (Ben) and East (me + overflow pool). Please see the routing column in hotlist_priority_20270604.xlsx; it's pre-populated for obvious owners.
- Acceptance criteria for a claim: SDR must leave a logged activity in HubSpot with the template \"WEBINAR_H_CLAIM\" + one-sentence discovery note. If no activity after 48 hours, auto-escalate to me and Marissa.
